senses.definition: Describing a molecule or protein that naturally holds itself in an inactive state by blocking its own activity.
examples: In its resting form, the enzyme remains autoinhibited until a signal activates it.
The receptor is autoinhibited by one of its own domains, which prevents unwanted signaling.

senses.definition: Able to be spread or transferred within the same person by moving infected material from one body site to another.
examples: Some skin lesions are autoinoculable and can appear at new sites after scratching.
Warts are highly autoinoculable and often spread along shaving lines.

senses.definition: A medical procedure in which cells, tissue, or material taken from a person’s own body are prepared and then injected or implanted back into that same person.
examples: Autoinoculation can be used to boost a patient’s immune response using their own cells.
The treatment involved autoinoculation with modified tumor cells.

senses.definition: The spread of an infection from one part of a person’s body to another part of the same body, usually by touching or scratching.
examples: Frequent scratching may lead to autoinoculation of the virus to nearby skin.
Patients are advised how to avoid autoinoculation of warts to other areas.

senses.definition: Relating to a person’s own intellectual activity, interests, or self‑directed thinking, often in an overly self‑absorbed way.
examples: The novel has a strongly autointellectual tone that focuses on the narrator’s inner debates.
His autointellectual style of conversation can seem distant from everyday concerns.

senses.definition: A substance produced within the body that can poison or harm that same body.
examples: The researchers identified an autointoxicant formed during abnormal metabolism.
In some disorders, waste products act as autointoxicants and damage tissues.

senses.definition: Poisoning of the body caused by toxic substances that are produced within the body itself.
examples: The theory of autointoxication once linked many diseases to toxins formed in the intestines.
Symptoms were thought to result from autointoxication rather than external infection.

senses.definition: Plural of autointoxication: cases or types of poisoning caused by toxins produced inside the body.
examples: Early medical writers described many supposed autointoxications affecting the nervous system.
Modern research has clarified which autointoxications have a real physiological basis.

senses.definition: A process in which atoms or molecules of the same kind react with each other so that some lose electrons and others gain them, forming ions without any outside substance.
examples: The autoionisation of water produces a very small number of hydrogen and hydroxide ions.
Autoionisation plays an important role in the behavior of certain liquids at high temperatures.

senses.definition: Plural of autoionisation: several instances or types of self‑ionisation in pure substances.
examples: Different autoionisations can be compared by looking at their equilibrium constants.
The textbook discusses autoionisations in water and liquid ammonia.

senses.definition: The same as autoionisation, especially in American English: the self‑ionisation of a pure substance without any added chemicals.
examples: Autoionization of water is essential for understanding acid–base chemistry.
At higher temperatures, the autoionization of the solvent increases.

senses.definition: Plural of autoionization: multiple cases or types of self‑ionisation reactions in pure substances.
examples: The article compares autoionizations in several protic solvents.
Different autoionizations show different temperature dependences.

senses.definition: Irrigation that operates automatically or uses a person’s own bodily fluids for washing a body cavity.
examples: The surgeon chose an autoirrigation system to flush the joint during the operation.
Modern fields may use autoirrigation controlled by soil‑moisture sensors.

senses.definition: A person who drives or travels in an automobile, especially as a regular or enthusiastic driver.
examples: The road was crowded with autoists heading for the coast.
Early autoists often had to repair their own cars on the roadside.

senses.definition: A machine that automatically forms pottery or ceramic pieces using a rotating mold.
examples: The factory replaced several manual wheels with an autojigger to speed production.
Using an autojigger, workers can produce identical plates in large numbers.

senses.definition: A very large or powerful motor vehicle that seems impossible to stop.
examples: The runaway truck was described as an autojuggernaut crashing down the highway.
In the story, an autojuggernaut plows through the deserted city streets.

senses.definition: A market or event where people buy and sell used car parts, tools, and related items, often outdoors.
examples: He picked up a rare carburetor at the weekend autojumble.
Classic car fans often search autojumbles for original parts.

senses.definition: Plural of autojumble: several events where used car parts and related goods are bought and sold.
examples: During the summer, there are autojumbles almost every weekend.
He travels around the country visiting different autojumbles.

senses.definition: Plural of autokinesis: multiple cases of spontaneous or voluntary movement, or repeated instances of the autokinetic illusion.
examples: The study recorded several autokineses in the muscles at rest.
Pilots were tested for their susceptibility to autokineses in dark‑room experiments.

senses.definition: Spontaneous or voluntary movement, especially movement that seems to start by itself in muscles or organs.
examples: Muscle autokinesis can occur in certain neurological conditions.
The patient complained of a strange sense of autokinesis in his limbs.

senses.definition: A visual illusion in which a small, fixed point of light in darkness appears to move.
examples: Autokinesis can cause a stationary star to appear to wander in the night sky.
Pilots are trained to recognize autokinesis and trust their instruments.

senses.definition: A variant form of autokinesis, meaning spontaneous or self‑generated movement.
examples: Older texts sometimes use the term autokinesy instead of autokinesis.
Autokinesy of the eye muscles may give rise to unusual sensations.

senses.definition: Relating to or showing autokinesis, especially the illusion of movement in a fixed point of light.
examples: The autokinetic effect can make a single light appear to drift in darkness.
Researchers used an autokinetic target in their perception experiments.

senses.definition: A supreme ruler or emperor with complete power, especially in historical Greek or Byzantine use.
examples: In Byzantine titles, the emperor was often styled basileus autokrator.
The term autokrator emphasizes the ruler’s absolute authority.

senses.definition: A laryngoscope that is designed to be used by the patient on themself or that works partly automatically.
examples: The device functions as an autolaryngoscope for remote throat examinations.
An autolaryngoscope can help patients monitor chronic laryngeal conditions at home.

senses.definition: Relating to or using an autolaryngoscope.
examples: Autolaryngoscopic techniques may enable home monitoring of vocal cord health.
The autolaryngoscopic images were transmitted securely to the clinic.

senses.definition: The examination of one’s own larynx using a mirror or laryngoscope, often with a specially designed device.
examples: Autolaryngoscopy allows motivated patients to observe their vocal folds directly.
The study evaluated the safety of autolaryngoscopy for trained singers.

senses.definition: A person who worships themself or is excessively devoted to their own interests.
examples: The philosopher described the selfish ruler as an autolater serving only his own glory.
Critics saw the celebrity as an autolater obsessed with her public image.

senses.definition: Plural of autolatry: several acts or systems of worshipping oneself.
examples: Modern culture shows many subtle autolatries of the individual self.
The essay traces historical autolatries in literature and art.

senses.definition: The worship of oneself; extreme self‑admiration or self‑devotion described as if it were a religion.
examples: He accused the movement of promoting a dangerous autolatry of personal success.
Her writing mocks the quiet autolatry of social media culture.

senses.definition: Washing out a body cavity or wound using a solution that flows automatically, often recirculating or self‑draining.
examples: Autolavage of the joint space was maintained throughout the surgery.
The device allows continuous autolavage without manual suction.

senses.definition: A wound or injury that a person causes to their own body.
examples: The psychiatrist documented repeated autolesion of the patient’s arms.
Autolesion can be a sign of severe emotional distress.

senses.definition: Relating to processes that take place within a lake or other standing body of fresh water, without outside input.
examples: Autolimnetic processes can change the oxygen levels at different depths of the lake.
The study focused on autolimnetic nutrient cycling in small ponds.

senses.definition: A rock fragment or crystal within an igneous rock that formed from the same magma as the surrounding material.
examples: The geologist identified several autoliths embedded in the basalt flow.
Autoliths can reveal changes in the magma chamber over time.

senses.definition: To make a lithograph by drawing directly on the stone or plate yourself, rather than having someone else do it.
examples: The artist chose to autolithograph all the prints in the series.
She preferred to autolithograph her designs to keep full control over the image.

senses.definition: An artist or printer who creates lithographs by drawing the images personally on the stone or plate.
examples: As an autolithographer, he insisted on handling every stage of the process.
The museum displayed works by several famous autolithographers.

senses.definition: Relating to lithographs drawn and prepared by the artist personally.
examples: The portfolio consists of ten autolithographic plates.
Autolithographic work often shows a direct, expressive line.

senses.definition: The process of making lithographs by drawing directly on the stone or plate yourself.
examples: Autolithography became popular among artists who wanted more control over reproduction.
The workshop offers courses in autolithography and etching.

senses.definition: In computing, to load a program component or file automatically when it is needed, without the user doing anything.
examples: The framework will autoload the class when you first use it.
You can configure the script to autoload settings on startup.

senses.definition: A device or program that loads something automatically, such as shells into a gun or files into computer memory.
examples: The tank uses an autoloader to feed shells into the main gun.
PHP projects often rely on an autoloader to include class files.

senses.definition: Plural of autoloader: several automatic loading devices or programs.
examples: Modern tanks with autoloaders can operate with smaller crews.
Different frameworks provide their own autoloaders for handling classes.

senses.definition: The act or process of loading something automatically, especially in weapons or software.
examples: Autoloading improves the rate of fire but adds mechanical complexity.
The language supports autoloading of classes based on their names.

senses.definition: Designed to load automatically without manual action.
examples: They tested a new autoloading rifle on the range.
An autoloading script can bring in all required libraries.

senses.definition: Describing a word that has the property it names, such as the word 'short' being short.
examples: The word 'English' is autological because it is an English word.
Philosophers like to play with autological and heterological adjectives.

senses.definition: Plural of autology: several cases or examples of words that describe themselves.
examples: Students were asked to list autologies such as 'unhyphenated'.
Logical paradoxes often involve autologies and their opposites.

senses.definition: A word that describes itself, or a person who studies or favors self‑describing words, depending on context.
examples: Some writers use 'autologist' to mean a self‑describing adjective.
As an autologist, she collects words whose forms reflect their meanings.

senses.definition: In medicine, coming from the same person, especially when tissue, cells, or blood are taken from a patient and given back to that same patient.
examples: The patient received an autologous bone marrow transplant.
Autologous blood donation reduces the risk of immune reactions.

senses.definition: The property of a word that describes itself, or the study of such words.
examples: In autology, 'polysyllabic' is an example because it has many syllables.
Autology raises puzzles about how language refers to itself.

senses.definition: Light produced by a substance or organism itself, without heat from outside and without obvious external energy.
examples: Scientists observed autoluminescence in the decaying plant material.
Autoluminescence can interfere with sensitive optical measurements.

senses.definition: Emitting light by itself, without needing an external light source or high heat.
examples: The autoluminescent crystals glowed faintly in the dark.
Certain autoluminescent organisms are used as natural light sources in research.

senses.definition: A mixture that results when cells or tissues break themselves down using their own enzymes.
examples: The yeast autolysate provides vitamins and amino acids for the culture medium.
Researchers prepared a cell autolysate to analyze intracellular proteins.

senses.definition: Plural of autolysate: several self‑digested cell or tissue extracts.
examples: Different yeast autolysates are used to flavor soups and sauces.
The lab compared autolysates from various bacterial strains.

senses.definition: To break down by the action of a cell’s own enzymes, or to allow such self‑digestion to happen.
examples: After death, cells begin to autolyse and their contents leak out.
The dough is left to autolyse so that the flour fully hydrates.
